questions.question: Explain the difference between a process and a program with an example.
keyPoints: A program is a passive entity (static file) stored on disk
A process is an active entity in execution with its own memory space
Same program can have multiple processes running simultaneously
Example: Ten users running Microsoft Word have one program but ten processes

questions.question: What is a Process Control Block (PCB)? List at least four information stored in it.
keyPoints: PCB is a kernel data structure containing process information
Stores: Process ID, Process state, Program counter, CPU registers
Also stores: Memory management info, Accounting data, I/O status information
Essential for context switching between processes

questions.question: Describe the five basic process states and explain when a process moves from one state to another.
keyPoints: NEW: Process being created by OS
READY: Process waiting in queue for CPU allocation
RUNNING: Process instructions being executed by CPU
WAITING: Process waiting for I/O or event
TERMINATED: Process finished execution
Transitions: New→Ready, Ready→Running, Running→Ready (preemption), Running→Waiting (I/O request), Waiting→Ready (I/O completion), Running→Terminated

questions.question: What is a context switch? What is its significance in process scheduling?
keyPoints: Context switch is saving the state of running process and loading another's state
Involves storing CPU registers, program counter, and other CPU-specific data to PCB
Significance: Enables multiprogramming by allowing CPU to serve multiple processes
Has overhead (dispatch latency) - time spent switching reduces useful CPU work

questions.question: Differentiate between CPU-bound and I/O-bound processes with examples.
keyPoints: CPU-bound processes spend most time doing computations with long CPU bursts
I/O-bound processes spend more time waiting for I/O with short CPU bursts
Examples: CPU-bound - video encoding, scientific calculations; I/O-bound - file copying, web browsing
I/O-bound processes yield CPU frequently causing more context switches

questions.question: Explain the terms zombie process and orphan process.
keyPoints: Zombie: Terminated child process whose parent hasn't called wait() to collect exit status
Zombie remains in process table consuming kernel resources until parent collects status
Orphan: Child process that continues running after parent terminates
Orphans in Unix are adopted by init process (PID 1) which performs wait() on them

questions.question: Explain the process creation mechanism in Unix-like operating systems using fork() and exec() system calls. How does this differ from process creation in Windows?
keyPoints: Unix: fork() creates child process as exact copy of parent (duplicates address space)
exec() replaces the child process image with new program
fork()+exec() combination creates new process running different program
Windows: CreateProcess() does both creation and program loading in single call
Windows passes command-line arguments and many attributes in one function call
Both approaches achieve same goal but with different system call interfaces

questions.question: Explain the concept of process scheduling. Discuss the three levels of schedulers and their roles.
keyPoints: Process scheduling decides which process gets CPU and when
Three levels: Long-term (job scheduler) - selects processes from job queue to ready queue
Controls degree of multiprogramming, balances I/O and CPU-bound processes
Medium-term (swapper) - temporarily removes processes from memory to backing store
Short-term (CPU scheduler) - selects from ready queue for immediate execution
Must be fast (milliseconds) as invoked frequently, affects system performance significantly

questions.question: Define turnaround time, waiting time, and response time. How are these metrics used to evaluate CPU scheduling algorithms?
keyPoints: Turnaround time: Completion time minus arrival time (total time in system)
Waiting time: Time spent in ready queue (turnaround time minus CPU burst time)
Response time: Time from arrival to first CPU allocation
Lower values indicate better performance
Scheduling algorithms aim to minimize average turnaround time and waiting time
Different algorithms prioritize different metrics based on system goals
FCFS favors long burst processes, SJF minimizes average waiting time, Round Robin improves response time

questions.question: Describe the process state transition diagram in detail. Explain all possible state transitions with causes for each transition.
keyPoints: NEW→READY: OS admits process after initialization
READY→RUNNING: CPU scheduler dispatches ready process
RUNNING→READY: Preemption by scheduler or time slice expiry
RUNNING→WAITING: Process initiates blocking I/O or waits for event
WAITING→READY: I/O completion or event occurs
RUNNING→TERMINATED: Process completes normally or aborts
Additional: READY→WAITING not valid, WAITING→RUNNING not possible directly
Multiple processes can be in READY and WAITING states, only one in RUNNING (single CPU)
